hook: You already know how to multiply matrices and solve systems of equations — but have you ever wondered what a matrix *does* geometrically, beyond just shuffling numbers around? The missing piece is the concept of a linear transformation: a structure-preserving map between vector spaces that reveals the true geometric soul of every matrix. What does it really mean for two vector spaces to be, in essence, the same?
concept: Every topic in this unit circles around one powerful idea: linear transformations are the natural 'maps' between vector spaces, and studying them — their structure, their failures to be invertible, and how they look in different coordinate systems — is the same as understanding linear algebra itself. Formally, we study the structure of homomorphisms between vector spaces.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to recognize and verify linear transformations by checking the two defining properties $T(u+v)=T(u)+T(v)$ and $T(cu)=cT(u)$, and explore a rich variety of examples including rotations, projections, and differentiation.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: The Matrix of a Linear Transformation
topic_roadmap.description: Master how to construct the matrix that represents any linear transformation with respect to chosen bases, making abstract maps concretely computable.
You will learn to compute the kernel and image of a linear transformation and use the Rank-Nullity Theorem to understand how a transformation compresses or spans its target space.
Apply the concept of an isomorphism to determine when two vector spaces are structurally identical, and appreciate why every $n$-dimensional space is essentially $\mathbb{R}^n$.
You will learn to compose linear transformations, translate composition into matrix multiplication, and determine precisely when a transformation is invertible.
Master how the matrix of a single linear transformation changes when you switch bases, and understand the role of similarity transformations $B = P^{-1}AP$.
You will explore the dual space of linear functionals and discover how every linear transformation induces a dual transformation acting in the opposite direction between dual spaces.
